speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 10, 1997. We are conducting an interview in English with Clara Heller Isaacman in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. My name is Irene Dansky. And we are conducting an interview with Clara Heller Isaacman at her home in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, on January 10, 1997. The interview will be conducted in English. Would you tell us your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Clara Heller Isaacman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. C-L-A-R-A capital H-E-L-L-E-R capital I-S-A-A-C-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell us, please, where and when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a particular city-- what city where you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city of Borsa, B-O-R-S-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not going to give you my date. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [LAUGHTER] How long did you live in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Romania-- I don't remember Romania. I must have been a year or a year and a half before my daddy decided to-- no, I was more than a year and a half-- four years old-- before my daddy decided to leave Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why did he leave Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left Romania-- he used to tell me a story that he left Romania because of anti-Semitism. He was Romanian born and raised there. And he went to vote. And he had to cross a bridge. And every time he crossed that bridge to vote once a year, he used to be beaten up. And the last time, he was very sick and came home, practically nearly killed because he went to vote. And he decided that he didn't want his children to be brought up in a country like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they move?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to Antwerp, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in your family at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Heshie, my sister-- and my younger brother wasn't born in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Romania. He was born in Hungary. Because my mother went to visit, I understand, her family. And that's what happened. But we had lived already in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's full name was Shalom, S-H-O-L-O-M, Heller, H-E-L-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose, R-O-S-A, Halpert, H-A-L-P-E-R-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in Antwerp, was there anybody in your household besides your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a helper, somebody who lived in with us, a maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your childhood like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood, I remember, was very pleasant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parents were-- we were upper-middle class. My daddy was in the diamond business because everybody in Antwerp was in the diamond business. But he also had a soda factory, the only soda factory in Antwerp. And I remember it very well. It was-- we had the house and then a garden, a little garden, and then the factory in the back. I went to Hebrew school. I went to public school. I went to camps. I belonged to youth movement. And I had a very lovely education. I also remember my parents, who, even they had-- they didn't always give me. I had to work for it. I had to do something for it. I think that's why I survived because they showed me the value of work. My friends were Jewish friends and non-Jewish friends. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My best friend was a non-Jewish friend, who later on-- I will talk about it. Maybe I should right now-- but disappointed me, because when Hitler came in, she didn't want to see me anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, about what time-- about what year did your family move to Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family must have moved to Antwerp-- I really don't know exactly. But I would say in 1926, '27, or '28, between those three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so long before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there special holidays? Were the Jewish holidays celebrated in a special way in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an Orthodox Jew. So the, the celebration was according to the Bible, what you do according to Jewish laws. My mother was a worldly woman. And she bended. She didn't always follow exactly. But I remember our Sabbath. We had, every Friday night, a beautiful table set with the best china. My daddy went to synagogue. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When he came home, my mother had lit the candles. And we had a Sabbath. We sang. We prayed. And we-- the family was together. That evening, nobody could have separated us but Hitler, who did later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the holidays, Passover or Purim-- or any special things you remember about any of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during Purim, of course, dressing up and going to Hebrew school, and wanting to, to be-- win the first prize as the Queen Esther. But I, I don't think I ever won it. And also, Passover-- during Passover, my daddy always had strangers at our Seder, those who couldn't have a Seder. And I remember a lot of people in our home. And I also remember very well when we opened the door for Elijah during Passover, my daddy used to say, maybe somebody else doesn't have a room. And maybe somebody will walk in. Won't be Elijah, but it could be somebody who needs a meal. The other holidays were Hanukkah-- were full of gifts. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my Christian friends and my Jewish friends, we exchanged gifts. But we never associated and never said it's the same holiday. Because we knew it was much-- it was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- one day, I asked my daddy, can I go to church? I would like to see how they are celebrating Christmas. My daddy was hesitant. He didn't want me to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother convinced him. And the only thing I remember-- and it rings in my ears-- he said, don't kneel. And Antwerp has a beautiful cathedral. And I went to midnight mass. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I celebrated with them. Of course, I did not kneel. But I will never forget the holiness there that those people felt. I didn't. To me, it was something that I was learning. I was a Jew, and I should know this, too. And that's what my mother told-- convinced my daddy, that I should go and see it. And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went home very satisfied that I was able to see somebody else's holiday, how they are celebrating it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your family was in Antwerp at that point, was there a big Jewish community in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The community was divided, very Orthodox and conservative. There were no reformed Jews, as I would put it now. And the community was one, though, when it came to anything to help, to do, to celebrate. It was a very big Jewish community. As a matter of fact, before Hitler, there were 50,000 Jews in Antwerp. And 5,000 remained alive. And I am one of the 5,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish community fit in well with the rest of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as far as I know. Because I-- the only thing I can remember is the school, a public school, where my mother and all of us mixed with everybody. And I didn't feel anti-Semitism. I remember my street. It wasn't all Jewish, but most of them were Jews. And I didn't feel that I was different, better, or somebody else was not as good-- and my teachers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The rest, I remember my daddy having partners who were not Jewish. And I never heard him say anything derogatory about them. Or if he did, I didn't hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any experiences of anti-Semitism other than in the school, something that was in the newspaper or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't think so. The only thing I know that-- I was brought up from when I was six or seven, I belonged to a youth group, to a Zionist youth group. Each one of us in my house belonged to different youth groups. One was more Orthodox. One was socialistic, and so on. And we, we-- our goal was once, when we grow up, to see Palestine then, of course, and maybe to live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friends, and my ideals, and my, my looking to my future of my life, was one day to be with Jews. And there was no other question. Even if I stay in Antwerp, I will-- my dearest friends and my nearest friends will be Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was with them in the-- I went to camp with them in the summer. I played with them. I hiked with them. I was-- the house was always full of children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get your family organized chronologically, OK-- the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was my brother, my older brother. There were first, Clara, then Heshie, then Frieda, and then Elie.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry. What language was spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- my mother and father spoke Hungarian and Romanian. They didn't want us to-- when they didn't want us to understand something, that's what they did. And the language that we spoke was Yiddish. When I went to class, and school, and on the streets, we spoke Dutch, Flemish. And we also spoke French.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the changes in what was going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. Because in my home, we spoke about the changes. And there was-- I heard my mother and father speaking about changes. And I heard them speak about Germany and what's happening in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a matter of fact, in 1939, my daddy brought home a 16-year-old boy or so. And to me, he was like a big hero or something. And my mother said, where are your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was explaining at the table, at the dinner table, that he didn't know where his parents were and that he left. His parents gave them enough money to leave Germany because they were afraid of his life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My daddy always used to bring home-- my daddy went to shul every day in the morning to pray. And he always used to bring home people for dinner, also, in the evening. And my mother-- and that's why I remember it very well-- was upset that day that she didn't have such a big meal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, why didn't you tell me you were going to bring somebody home? And my daddy said, so give him my, my food. I'll eat something else. And this is how I was brought up, my home. How did you become aware of what was going on, besides visitors?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the youth group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One night, we had a meeting. We had an ulam, U-L-A-M, a meeting room. And our leader was telling us about what Hitler is doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was saying that maybe one day, all of us will have to leave even Antwerp, Belgium. And he told us about the Youth, Y-O-U-T-H, Aliyah, A-L-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how would I spell Aliyah? A-L-O? I.

speaker: interviewee
text: A-L-I-- my god, I can't spell anymore-- Y-A, Aliyah. And they were planning already, the-- in, in Palestine then, there were leaders who were planning to bring to Palestine young boys and girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that-- when was--

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, my leader, who was in charge of us in Antwerp, left end of 1939 with the Youth Aliyah. First, they were going to France. Then they were going to Switzerland. And then they were smuggled into Palestine. That was one of the stops. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wanted to go. My brother wanted to go. My mother never-- she said, you're so young. We-- I can't let you go. And in 1941, one group went. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they caught them on the street. They stopped to eat with the-- across the street a German barrack. And the Germans caught them. And they were all killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to 1938 for a minute. How did your parents feel about being in Antwerp at that time? Did they feel secure?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very safe. They, they-- my daddy-- I heard my daddy say many, many times. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle came from Czechoslovakia in 1939. And he begged my father to go to Palestine with him. And I remember my uncle-- my father saying, it won't happen in Belgium, not in Belgium. And, as a matter of fact, I remember my uncle very well because he-- was my first watch. He brought me a little watch. And he left. And he, from Czechoslovakia-- he was in the furniture business-- and he hired a plane. And he filled the plane with all his belongings. And they flew to Palestine. And this is one of my mother's brothers. My mother had seven brothers, one that remained alive and has children in Israel now and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emil Halpert, E-M-E-L-I, Halpert, H-L-P-E-R-T. I have to see the word. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. OK. What gave your parents a sense of security?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had trust in the Belgium-- people. And I think they had trust in the king that, later on, disappointed everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents belong to any political groups at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Zionist group was a political group. It was a socialist-- but I was too young to feel that it's a political group. I knew it was a group that is for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any other youth groups at all, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged to the scouts. As a matter of fact, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one-- when Hitler already had occupied us, and I marched with the scouts, they pointed at me to the Hitler unit. We have a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things begin to change in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time was in end of 1941 or in, in middle of 1941, when we have to-- had to wear our Jewish star. And the Jews were not allowed to go out after 6 o'clock. The businesses were taken away from a lot of Jews. And gradually, systematically, Hitler did that for at least a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family try to leave Antwerp at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, twice. My family-- when the Germans invaded in 1940, my daddy had a car. And he filled the car. And we drove to France. But we were late. And when we got to the borders of France, already they didn't let us in. And we had to turn back and come back home. The second time-- and wasn't much later after that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were still boats going to England from Ostend, O-S-T-E-N-D-E. And my daddy took us all. And we went to Ostend. When we got to Ostend, the last boat had left five minutes before we got there. In Ostend, the Germans were bombing. A lot of bombs were bombing Ostend. And they bombing the-- they were trying to bomb as much of the English soldiers who were there, and the English army, and whatever. And I was hit by a bomb. I have on my back a little-- for life. And-- but you don't feel it. See, the bombs were falling and falling. And my sister was near me. And I heard the bomb coming. And I lie down on top of her. I wanted to protect her. And I did. But I was hit. And I didn't even know I was hit. All at once, when it stopped, the bombing stopped, my mother gave a shout, Clara's bleeding! And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they took us into a place. And the only thing I remember is that it smelled from a lot of blood. And it smelled from a lot of iodine and all kinds of things. And there was a priest there. And the priest took care of me. And he kept saying to my mother, don't worry. She's not hurt too much. She'll be all right. And he gave me the piece of shrapnel that hurt me-- that had hit me. And then we saw that we have to go back home again. And we started going back home. Now, when we started going back home, we didn't have any more shoes. I walked with burlap on my feet. And the car-- was no more car because we didn't have gas. So my daddy exchanged the car for a horse. And-- but even the horse, he couldn't feed. And we were very, very tired on the way home. And we stopped on a farm. And when we stopped on the farm, my father still had enough money. And they asked them to-- if we could sleep over and to buy some food. And they were giving us. And we sat down. And the farm was so dirty, and so-- but we sat down to eat. And my mother said to us, go wash your hands. And this was the first time that I really felt anti-Semitism. I didn't know it was anti-Semitism. But I felt different.

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman there said, I thought the Jews are dirty. Why do you have to go and wash your hands? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother looked at her and said, we wash hands before we eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't make an issue out of it. And they were nice to us until the next morning till we left. But that 'the Jews are dirty' remained with me for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did neighbors around react?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back, first of all, a lot of our friends, Jewish friends, had left. Some of them were caught by the Germans going I don't know where. And then the rest of the neighbors who remained with us-- the non-Jewish neighbors didn't talk to us, just like we didn't exist anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jewish neighbors, we used to get together in the evenings in this home or in that home. And I used to hear them talk-- what next? Now they realized that it was maybe not too late, but it was too late. Because they didn't leave when the invasion came. And like us, we tried twice to get away. And we couldn't anymore. How about school? Were you still in school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would-- they would let us-- they let us to go back to school. And I have a few incidents that happened in school. One was-- of course, we had to wear out a yellow star. And one day, we came in. And our teacher looked at the window, looked outside. And she had just given us pieces of paper, like propaganda paper. And she gave them to everybody. And there were a-- quite a few, I would say five, six, still Jewish children in school in my class. There were more. And she said to all of us, I see Germans coming. And you better eat the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was on the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the paper was anti-German propaganda. We hadn't even read it. She wanted us to give this to our parents. And we all ate the paper. When the Germans came up-- it took us maybe 5 or 10 minutes. I can't believe if you would tell me [LAUGHS] now that I did it, but I know I did it. And when the Germans walked in, and they had Fraulein-- and they were very-- of course, the Germans were very sweet in the class. And they looked around. And they couldn't find anything. And they said to her, well, we were told to come into your class. And when they left, she said, you saved my life. The other incident was when I really started feeling that I am not going to-- I'm not the same. I'm different. As I came to school like every other day, and the teacher said she has an announcement to make. All Jewish children, take your books home because this is your last day of school. You cannot come to school anymore. And I refused to take my books home, not that I-- big deal. But I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I cried. And I said, why? What's different? Why am I not allowed to? And my mother and father said, don't worry. It's going to get better. About how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the first grade or a second grade. I think I was second grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you going to any other kind of school, any private or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a Hebrew school. I used to go Wednesday and Sunday to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that allowed to continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for awhile. And I will tell you a story about what they did with the Hebrew school.
